Name: AdaptiveLayerSkippingForLatencyConstrainedInference
Title: Adaptive Layer-Skipping for Latency-Constrained Inference in Transformers
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training-free, adaptive layer-skipping mechanism, guided by real-time activation geometry and spectral stability metrics, can significantly reduce inference latency in Transformer models while maintaining high accuracy and robustness under distributional shifts.
Related Work: Recent works such as KnapSpec and DeeAD have explored layer-skipping in Transformers to reduce inference latency. However, these methods often rely on static heuristics or costly dynamic programming algorithms. Our proposal introduces a training-free, adaptive mechanism that leverages real-time activation geometry and spectral stability metrics to dynamically skip layers, providing a more flexible and efficient approach.
Abstract: Transformer models, while powerful, suffer from high inference latency, limiting their real-time deployment. We propose a training-free, adaptive layer-skipping framework that leverages real-time activation geometry and spectral stability metrics to dynamically skip layers during inference. By computing the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states, our method identifies well-conditioned subspaces that are robust to layer-skipping. We enforce strict latency budgets using a dynamic compute-throttling scheduler that adjusts the number of layers to process based on the subspace stability. Our approach is theoretically grounded in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under perturbation. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across adversarial benchmarks and natural domain shifts, our method demonstrates superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers compared to static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines.
Experiments: 1. **Layer-Skipping Policy Evaluation**: Measure the impact of different layer-skipping policies (static, dynamic, and our adaptive approach) on inference latency and accuracy using Transformer models on WILDS benchmarks. Use metrics such as wall-clock time, FLOPs, and accuracy to compare performance.

2. **Spectral Stability Analysis**: Analyze the spectral condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states in real-time during inference. Correlate these metrics with the robustness of the model to layer-skipping and distributional shifts. Use PCA and randomized SVD to compute these metrics.

3. **Dynamic Compute-Throttling**: Implement a dynamic compute-throttling scheduler that adjusts the number of layers to process based on the subspace stability. Evaluate the scheduler's ability to enforce strict latency budgets while maintaining high accuracy.

4. **Adversarial Robustness**: Test the robustness of our method under adversarial conditions and out-of-distribution (OOD) contexts. Measure the accuracy and latency of the model under these conditions and compare it to baseline methods.

5. **Ablation Study**: Conduct an ablation study to understand the contributions of different components of our framework, such as the spectral stability metrics and the dynamic compute-throttling scheduler.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. **Computational Overhead of Real-Time Metrics**: Computing spectral stability metrics in real-time may introduce additional computational overhead. We will carefully optimize these computations to ensure they remain within acceptable latency bounds.

2. **Generalization Across Models and Tasks**: The effectiveness of our method may vary across different Transformer architectures and tasks. We will evaluate our method on a diverse set of models and benchmarks to ensure its generalizability.

3. **Robustness to Noisy Inputs**: The method may be sensitive to noisy inputs, which could affect the accuracy of the spectral stability metrics. We will investigate techniques to mitigate this sensitivity.
